entries.name: Supply or Die
entries.patch: Introduced in Alpha 4.0.2 (PTU Feb 2025; Live 28 Feb 2025). Ran as a limited-time Pyro event into ~late March 2025. Not present in the current 4.8.x live build.
entries.location: Pyro system, with narrative ties to Stanton. Mission objectives and drop-offs centered on Pyro stations (e.g., Pyro Gateway, Ruin Station / Checkmate-area locations) plus Stanton Gateway for certain faction deliveries.
entries.overview: Supply or Die was a limited-time, faction-based event built around the lawless Pyro system, layered on top of the ongoing narrative in which the Slicers gang was exposed as the extremist, anti-Pyro Frontier Fighters. With the region destabilized, two organizations competed to stockpile materiel and players had to pick a side. The Citizens for Prosperity (CFP) took the lawful route, mining and gathering industrial resources and the rare chemical compound Detatrine to rebuild depleted reserves and produce medical supplies to stabilize the system. The Headhunters took the aggressive route, acquiring salvage and mining resources by any means, including combat and piracy, to rearm. In practice the event was a blend of high-risk hauling, mining, salvage, and PvP: traders moved commodities along contested routes, pirates ambushed them, and combat pilots escorted or raided the supply lines. Completing missions for your chosen faction accumulated event points that unlocked three reward tiers. It was a one-time event and is no longer running on the live servers.
objectives: Pick a faction: Citizens for Prosperity (lawful supply, mining, Detatrine) or Headhunters (salvage, combat, piracy)
Accept event resupply contracts from the Contract Manager app in your mobiGlas while in the Pyro system
Complete Salvage missions in Small, Medium, or Large tiers (collect progressively larger volumes of recyclable material/RMC)
Complete Mining & Trading missions: source listed commodities/minerals such as Tin, Ice/compressed Ice, copper and corundum and deliver them to the designated station
Run Detatrine and commodity delivery legs to the assigned drop-off (Pyro Gateway, Ruin, or other Pyro stations; Stanton Gateway for some deliveries)
Bank event points across missions to cross the three reward thresholds (roughly 1,470 points to reach all three tiers)
rewards: Tier 1: Decorative hangar diorama (cosmetic display item)
Tier 2: Ravager-212 "Outcast" Twin Shotgun
Tier 3: "Outcast" Paint Pack for the MISC Fortune, Drake Vulture and MISC Prospector
In-mission payouts: substantial aUEC from medium/large hauling, salvage and delivery contracts (organized crews reported earning very large sums over the run)
hazards: PvP and piracy: contested Pyro routes meant transporters were frequently ambushed; cargo could be stolen or destroyed in transit
Always scan with your radar before landing at or leaving a station; many players were caught out by ignoring contacts
Cargo-handling jank: offload crates one at a time rather than relying on quick-transfer, which could fail or void cargo
Avoid loitering near elevators and high-traffic hangar zones where crates and ships could be lost or destroyed
Commodity scarcity: copper and corundum kiosks were often bought out/monopolized by other players, stalling trade missions
Mining objectives (compressed Ice, Tin) gave little in-game guidance on where to source the materials
tips: Group up: solo play was punishing, while a squad could split escort, hauling and combat duties
For points-per-effort, focus on the mission types that paid the best ratio; consistent medium and large missions were the fastest path to all three tiers
Org/party members could share mission progress, speeding up point accumulation
Plan routes to dodge known pirate chokepoints rather than flying the direct line
Bring fit-for-purpose ships: Vulture (solo) or Reclaimer (crew) for salvage; Prospector (solo) or Mole (crew) for mining; Caterpillar, Hull C or Starfarer for bulk hauling
Decide your faction up front and stick to the matching mission types to avoid wasted runs
steps: Travel to the Pyro system and open the Contract Manager in your mobiGlas to view the Supply or Die event contracts
Choose your allegiance: Citizens for Prosperity for lawful mining/supply and Detatrine, or Headhunters for salvage and aggressive acquisition
Accept a mission tier that matches your loadout (salvage, mining/trade, or delivery); medium and large variants yield the most points
Acquire the required goods: salvage wrecks for RMC, mine minerals like Tin and Ice, or buy commodities such as copper/corundum from trade kiosks
Haul the cargo to the assigned drop-off station (e.g., Pyro Gateway, Ruin, or Stanton Gateway), scanning for hostiles before approach and at departure
Deliver and turn in to bank event points; repeat missions until you pass the Tier 1, Tier 2 and Tier 3 thresholds (~1,470 points total)
Claim the tiered rewards (diorama, Ravager-212 "Outcast" shotgun, "Outcast" paint pack) once each threshold is reached

entries.name: Frontier Fighters Finale
entries.location: Stanton and Pyro systems (outposts, bunkers, asteroid bases, and underground facilities)
entries.overview: A three-week narrative event that closed out the "Pyro Must Burn" storyline in Alpha 4.3.2 (launched October 17, 2025, ran until mid-November). Players took sides against Amelia Boyd's rogue Frontier Fighters by aligning with one of two patron factions and grinding contract missions for contribution points. Each of the three phases — Recon, Siege, and Takedown — ran on its own weekly schedule, awarded points independently (points did not carry between phases), and unlocked cosmetic rewards at fixed point thresholds. The Takedown phase culminated in a cooperative hunt against a Polaris capital ship. Because it was a time-limited narrative event tied to the 4.3.2 cycle, it is no longer live; it may recur in re-runs, but the dates and rewards below are for the original 4.3.2 run.
entries.access: Open to all players during the event window. Pick up faction contracts from the in-game contract/mobiGlas mission board after choosing a side; missions are available in both the Stanton and Pyro systems.
objectives: Choose a patron faction: Citizens for Prosperity (CFP, militia/humanitarian-leaning) or the Headhunters (outlaw-leaning). You may run missions for either or both.
Phase 1 (Recon): retrieve a datapad and deliver it to a station freight elevator; contracts involve either locating missing friendly operatives or hunting Frontier Fighter commanders.
Phase 2 (Siege): disrupt enemy regeneration and destroy key facilities via ship ambushes and on-foot sabotage (e.g. destroying medgel recharge units in aid shelters and underground facilities).
Phase 3 (Takedown): scan and destroy 10 beacon-marked outposts, then take part in the climactic Polaris capital-ship boss fight.
Earn contribution points each phase to reach the cosmetic reward thresholds before that phase ends.
steps: Open the contract board and accept a Frontier Fighters mission for your chosen faction (CFP or Headhunters). Missions are tagged by faction.
Recon week: fly to the target outpost or bunker, secure the datapad, and hand it in at the destination station's freight elevator. Stealth and speed are favored over open combat.

Siege week: run ship ambush contracts and ground sabotage runs. Space targets include light fighters, missile birds, and Cutlass Black-class enemies; ground targets are medgel recharge stations and regen facilities.
Takedown week: locate and destroy the 10 marked beacon outposts (avoid attacking prematurely, which could trigger bugs), then engage the Polaris.
Polaris fight: solo players whittle it down with hit-and-run torpedo strikes on the engines; groups assign scouts to jam sensors, bombers to hit weak points, and fighters to clear spawned defenders.
Track your contribution total and finish enough contracts to cross each phase's reward threshold before the weekly window closes.
rewards: Phase 1 (Recon): faction outfit at 1,500 contribution points — CFP tactical gear or a Headhunters-style rugged cloak/clothing
Phase 2 (Siege): a faction Greycat MTC vehicle paint at 1,750 contribution points — Liberation (CFP) or Reaper (Headhunters)
Phase 3 (Takedown): Anvil Arrow and Crusader Spirit ship paints/liveries (community sources tie these to clearing 10 takedowns / completing the final phase)
Per-mission Siege payouts (also grant points): Light Fighter ambush 24,000 aUEC / 50 pts; Missile Bird 44,000 aUEC / 75 pts; Cutlass Black 71,000 aUEC / 100 pts
hazards: The Polaris boss is a capital ship bristling with turrets and fighter bays; engaging it solo without hit-and-run discipline gets you destroyed quickly.
Pyro variants of the missions add asteroid bases with turret defenses, hostile enemy ships, and more random PvP/PvE encounters than the Stanton equivalents.
In Phase 3, attacking beacons prematurely or out of sequence was reported to cause bugs/objective failures.
Contribution points do NOT carry between phases — points earned in one week reset for the next, so you must hit each threshold within its own window.
As a time-limited event, all unearned rewards lock once the phase/event window closes.
tips: Run the event in Stanton rather than Pyro where possible: shorter travel times, more stable servers, fewer random encounters, and no reduced rewards versus Pyro.
Tailor your loadout per mission type — agile missile-armed ships for space ambushes, light armor with explosives for ground sabotage.
The high-value Siege contracts (Cutlass Black, 100 pts) are the most point-efficient toward the 1,750 threshold if you can handle the fight.
Group up for the Polaris hunt and split roles (sensor jamming, torpedo runs, spawn clearing); solo is possible but slow via repeated torpedo hit-and-runs on the engines.
You can earn rewards from both factions by running both contract lines, since you are not permanently locked to one side.

entries.name: Alliance Aid
entries.location: Levski (Nyx system) as the narrative hub, with contract activity spread across the Stanton system; player reports also note occasional Nyx/Pyro objectives
entries.overview: Alliance Aid (in-fiction framed as the "Lifeline for Levski" / "Clearing the Air" relief effort) was a large cooperative, progression-based event shipped with Star Citizen Alpha 4.6.0. It cast players as part of a relief coalition responding to a growing crisis around the Levski settlement, asking them to run a steady loop of repeatable supply-and-security contracts. Every completed contract paid normal aUEC plus event progress points, which fed both a server-wide community goal and per-category progression tracks that unlocked cosmetic and gameplay rewards. It was designed around three pillars — logistics, gathering, and combat — and explicitly rewarded teamwork, since grouped players each received full event points. As a release-window event it is no longer running on the current live build (Alpha 4.8.x); treat this entry as an archival walkthrough.
objectives: Hauling / Transport: move event cargo (relief supplies) between locations on timed delivery contracts, scaling from small-scale runs up through medium and large hauls
Collection / Acquisition: gather or recover required items and resources (including mining-style objectives) and deliver them with minimal backtracking
Mercenary / Defense: provide security for the relief effort — escort and defend NPC transport ships and clear hostiles so allied vessels can dock at remote sites
Feed both the shared community progress bar and your own per-category tracks until milestone rewards unlock
steps: Update to Alpha 4.6.0 and log in while the event is active (release-window only).
Open your mobiGlas Contracts / Mission Manager and look under the Priority tab, where all Alliance Aid contracts are grouped.
Accept a small, manageable stack of contracts of one type rather than spreading across many locations — fewer travel legs means fewer failure points.
Pick a category to focus: Transport (cargo hauls), Collection (gather/recover), or Mercenary/Defense (escort and combat).
Run that contract type as a repeatable loop — e.g. small cargo runs of roughly half a dozen crates on a short, predictable route — handing in cleanly each time.
Each hand-in awards aUEC plus event points that fill your category track (toward its 10% / 50% / 100% milestones) and contribute to the global community bar (30% / 70% / 90% / 100% milestones).
Switch mission category only when your current one becomes unreliable (congested landing pads, bugged steps), and run with a coordinated group when possible since each member earns full points.
rewards: aUEC payouts per contract, scaling with size — small cargo hauls reported around 40,000 aUEC; high-value transport/defense contracts reported up to roughly 588,000 aUEC (one delivery cited at ~400k+)
Global community-bar milestone rewards unlocked for everyone at 30%, 70%, 90% and 100% completion

Per-category track rewards at 10% / 50% / 100% completion, including apparel (e.g. division jackets for the transport and defense tracks), tractor-beam skins, ship liveries and special-edition weapons
A permanently account-linked MaxLift tractor-beam skin — reported as the first free pledge-level tractor beam, kept on the account permanently (including PTU access)
Defense-division special laser cannons (special-edition weapons)
A fabricator-type reward reported for completing all three category tracks (community-reported as ~30,000 total points / ~10,000 per track)
hazards: Landing-zone / pad congestion at popular hubs during peak times can stall cargo hand-ins and waste run time
Over-accepting contracts across many locations creates long travel legs, hand-in confusion, and high odds that one bugged step voids an entire run
Mercenary/Defense contracts involve hostile NPC ships and combat — losing the protected NPC transport can fail the contract; stay on the escorted asset rather than chasing extra kills
Listed credit reward does NOT track 1:1 with event points — some lower-paying missions give a better points-per-minute ratio, so chasing the biggest aUEC number can be inefficient for progression
Large-scale hauls (reported 45 min to an hour) can show diminishing point returns versus quick small runs
tips: Treat it as a contract loop, not a tour: log in, take a small clean stack, and repeat one reliable route.
Small cargo runs (around 6 crates) were reported to yield close to 800 event points in roughly 20 minutes — strong points-per-minute for early progress.
Because grouped players each get full event points (aUEC is shared, points are not), coordinated teams progress much faster — assign roles (pilot, gunner, logistics).
Prioritize consistency over perfection; a steady stream of small completed contracts beats occasional huge ones.
Combat/defense contracts become more worthwhile at the higher aUEC brackets (400k+); use them when transport pads are too congested.
Use a versatile mid-size cargo ship for short, predictable hops so you can pivot between hauling and light defense without re-equipping.

entries.name: Hathor Orbital Laser (Align & Mine)
entries.location: Stanton system: two sites in low orbit of Daymar (Attritus OLP + Lamina OLP, each with three ground PAFs) and two in low orbit of Aberdeen (Ruptura OLP + Vivere OLP, each with three ground PAFs).
entries.overview: A repeatable PvPvE sandbox activity built around the abandoned automated mining infrastructure of the dissolved Hathor Group. Each site pairs one Orbital Laser Platform (OLP) in space with a cluster of three ground-based Planetary Alignment Facilities (PAF). Players take over and realign the ground facilities, recover the hardware needed to restart the platform, power and fire the orbital laser down onto the moon's surface, then mine the rich mineral deposit (Carinite, including the high-value Pure variant) that the laser blast exposes. The recovered minerals are turned in to the Banu mission giver Wikelo as crafting materials toward exclusive ship, armor, and weapon rewards. Four sites exist: Attritus and Lamina over Daymar, Ruptura and Vivere over Aberdeen. The full activation chain (align all three facilities, then power and fire the platform) is a multi-stage loop spanning surface infiltration, ground combat, FPS puzzle/keycard work, and ship-based transport and boarding. Sites are occupied by the Nine Tails, so combat is expected throughout, and the exposed deposit is a contested, first-come resource shared with other players in the server.
objectives: Align all three Planetary Alignment Facilities (PAFs) belonging to a single Orbital Laser Platform (OLP) site
Recover the component boards ('comp boards') from the facilities' secured rooms
Recover the power banks / batteries needed to restart the orbital laser
Carry the batteries up to the OLP in orbit and restore its power
Fire the orbital laser at the surface to blast open a fresh crater/cave
Mine the exposed Carinite (especially the Pure variant) and extract it for turn-in
steps: Pick a target site. Each OLP works only with its own three matching PAFs: Attritus OLP with Attritus PAF-I/II/III and Lamina OLP with Lamina PAF-I/II/III over Daymar; Ruptura OLP with Ruptura PAF-I/II/III and Vivere OLP with Vivere PAF-I/II/III over Aberdeen.
Fly down to the first Planetary Alignment Facility on the moon's surface. Expect Nine Tails defenders; clear the immediate area before working the interior.

Find the blue-key printer in the facility buildings and print a blue access key. Use the blue key to reach the red-key printer, then print a red key to open the secured security, comms, and admin rooms.
Inside the secured rooms, collect the facility's component board(s) ('comp boards') and align/activate the site by interacting with its alignment console / communication dish.
Repeat the align-and-loot process at all three PAFs for that site, recovering one large battery/power bank from each (three total).
Load the three batteries onto a ship with a strong (max-lift) tractor beam and haul them up to the matching Orbital Laser Platform in low orbit.
Board the OLP (defended by additional Nine Tails) and insert the three batteries into the platform's power terminals to restore power.
Go to the bunker/control point directly beneath the laser and insert the activation card to fire the laser. The beam drills a fresh crater/cave into the moon surface below.
Travel to the newly blasted crater and mine the exposed deposit, focusing on Carinite and the Pure Carinite variant, using hand tools or a ROC/mining vehicle.
Extract and refine the ore, then deliver it to the Banu mission giver Wikelo to craft toward his exclusive reward catalogue.
rewards: Carinite ore, including the high-value Pure Carinite variant exposed only by firing the orbital laser
Materials usable with Wikelo (Banu mission giver) to craft exclusive rewards

Example Wikelo crafts (v4.8 community tracker): Constellation Taurus ship (~3 Pure Carinite + Wikelo Favor), ATLS GEO 'Cool Metal' (1 Pure Carinite + Wikelo Favor), Strata 'Heatwave' armor set (~30 Carinite among ingredients), TripleDown 'Heatwave' pistol (~20 Carinite among ingredients)
General facility loot from the secured rooms while clearing the sites
hazards: Nine Tails NPCs occupy both the ground PAFs and the orbital OLPs - expect FPS and ship combat at every stage
PvPvE: the exposed crater is a contested, first-come resource; other players may contest the deposit or the platform
Underground creatures ('Valaar worm'-type ambushers) reported attacking players during the mining phase (community-reported)
Long, multi-leg loop requiring both a combat-capable ship and a tractor-beam-capable hauler - vehicle loss mid-run can cost the whole attempt
Activation/'priming' bugs have been reported community-side where the laser fails to prime
tips: Tackle it as a group: one site needs ground clearing, keycard work, battery hauling, and orbital boarding - splitting roles is far faster and safer.
Bring a ship with a powerful (max-lift) tractor beam specifically for moving the three batteries up to the platform.
Work the keycard chain in order - blue-key printer first, then red-key printer - or you cannot reach the comp-board rooms.
Keep each OLP paired with its own three PAFs; batteries and alignment from one site do not activate another.
Have ROC mining gear or hand-mining tools and ore storage ready before you fire the laser, so you can extract the Carinite immediately before others arrive.
Check a current Wikelo reward tracker before grinding so you mine the right quantity of regular vs Pure Carinite for the specific ship/armor/weapon you want.

entries.name: Nine Tails Lockdown
entries.patch: Debuted Alpha 3.14.0; recurs as an evergreen dynamic event
entries.location: Stanton system — Crusader space. The blockade is anchored on CRU-L5 Beautiful Glen Station (the Lagrange rest stop the Nine Tails seize and ring with a quantum dampener).
entries.overview: Nine Tails Lockdown is a recurring server-wide dynamic event in which the Nine Tails syndicate seizes a Lagrange-point rest stop in Crusader space (CRU-L5 Beautiful Glen Station) and deploys a powerful quantum dampener that makes quantum travel impossible through the blockaded sector, choking off traffic and commerce. When it spins up, every player in the area receives an opening transmission about the station in distress, and a priority mission appears in the mobiGlas Contracts/General tab. Which side you can play depends on your CrimeStat. Lawful pilots (CrimeStat 0) sign on with Crusader Security to break the blockade; criminal pilots (CrimeStat 1+) get the opposite contract to fight for the syndicate. Because both factions of real players converge on the same contested point, the event blends scripted PvE waves with open PvP. It is time-limited each time it is active and is designed as an evergreen event that returns periodically rather than a one-time story beat.
steps: Be in or travel to Crusader space while the event is live. All players in range hear an opening transmission announcing a station in distress; a priority mission auto-populates in the mobiGlas Contracts (General/Personal) tab. Note that quantum travel through the locked-down sector is disabled, so you must sublight in from a nearby quantum marker.

Pick your side by CrimeStat. With CrimeStat 0 you receive the lawful Crusader Security contract; with CrimeStat 1 or higher you instead receive the unlawful Nine Tails contract. Accept the currently offered mission to join the fight.

Lawful, Phase 1 — 'Illegal Blockade: Seeking Nine Tails Fleet Responsible' (authorized by Director Sasha Rust, Crusader Security): escort and protect Crusader Security scanning ships as they sweep the area to pinpoint the hidden Nine Tails blockade fleet. Hold the line by destroying incoming waves of Nine Tails ships — and any criminal players defending them — so the security scanners survive long enough to locate the fleet.

Lawful, Phase 2 — 'Illegal Blockade: Neutralize Nine Tails Fleet': once the fleet's location is confirmed, a follow-up priority mission tasks you with finishing the job. Travel to the distress zone and help destroy the Nine Tails blockading fleet, which fields heavier resistance, to lift the dampener and reopen quantum travel.

Unlawful path: criminal players take the mirror objective — defend the Nine Tails fleet and destroy the Crusader Security ships and lawful players trying to break through, keeping the blockade and quantum lock in place for as long as possible.

Event resolves when one side wins control of the point. For lawful players, destroying the blockade fleet ends the lockdown, restores quantum travel to the sector, and completes the contract for payout and reputation.
objectives: Lawful: Protect Crusader Security scanner ships while they search for the Nine Tails fleet (Phase 1)
Lawful: Destroy incoming waves of Nine Tails ships and hostile (criminal) players
Lawful: Locate and destroy the Nine Tails blockading fleet to end the quantum lock (Phase 2)
Unlawful: Defend the Nine Tails fleet and destroy Crusader Security ships and lawful players to maintain the blockade
rewards: Crusader Security reputation for completing the lawful priority contracts (authorized by Director Sasha Rust)
aUEC payout on mission completion (exact amount varies by patch and is not reliably documented in current text sources)
Combat loot/salvage opportunities from the many destroyed ships at the contested point
hazards: Quantum travel is disabled inside the blockaded sector — you cannot quantum jump out of trouble; plan an escape and refuel/rearm runs accordingly
Open PvP zone: scripted Nine Tails PvE waves can instantly become PvP because real criminal players are fighting on the syndicate's side at the same point
High enemy ship density and concentrated waves can overwhelm a lone fighter; you take fire from both NPCs and players
Engaging on the lawful side near criminal players (and vice versa) can put you in the crossfire; mis-targeting friendly security ships risks a CrimeStat
Phase 2 throws heavier resistance than Phase 1 — expect a tougher final fleet fight
tips: Bring a capable combat ship with strong shields and bring (or pre-stage) extra missiles/ammo — the engagement is a sustained wave fight, not a single skirmish
Group up: the point is far easier and safer to hold with a wing or org backing you, especially against hostile players
Because you can't quantum out, stage a friendly rearm/repair ship or a nearby landing zone so you can resupply without abandoning the contract
Watch your CrimeStat before the event so you land on the side (lawful vs unlawful) you intend to play
Keep situational awareness on player ships, not just NPCs — the biggest threat is often another player joining the opposing faction
Stay close to the protected security scanners in Phase 1; letting them die can stall progress to the fleet-location phase

entries.name: Overdrive Initiative (Operation Overdrive)
entries.patch: Alpha 3.22.1 (building to the XenoThreat Global Event in Alpha 3.23.0)
entries.location: Stanton system (mission stages span planetary bunkers, deep space, and Security Post Kareah)
entries.overview: A multi-week, story-driven mission chain run by the Civilian Defense Force (CDF) in which everyday citizens are recruited to disrupt XenoThreat operations across Stanton before the faction can launch a full-scale assault. Introduced in Alpha 3.22.1, it was the fourth iteration of the XenoThreat dynamic-event series (following three Assault on Stanton runs) and served as the narrative and gameplay lead-in to the large XenoThreat Global Event that arrived with Alpha 3.23.0. Developers described it as an experiment to bring MMO-style linked quest chains and player-engagement mechanics into Star Citizen. It ran across roughly six connected missions grouped into five phases, mixing FPS (on-foot) and ship-combat objectives. In-fiction it was active from 2954-03-15 to 2954-05-10; it has since concluded and is no longer available. (Internal trivia: the chain was codenamed "Hex Penetrator," an anagram of "Pre XenoThreat" coined to throw off dataminers, which then accidentally stuck in dev docs.)
objectives: Phase 1 - INTEL RAID (5 stages, FPS): Raid XenoThreat-held bunkers, knock out hostile turrets and clear out XT troops, find and eliminate a target disguised as a civilian engineer, loot the key, then work a server room - reading the on-screen server numbers/codes and entering them to keep cooling systems running while an intel upload completes.

Phase 2 - PRIORITY TARGETS (3 stages, space): Hunt and destroy high-value XenoThreat bounty targets in space (HRT-tier), EVA to a marker to recover a key, and deliver it to the designated drop; the final stage introduces a Hammerhead.

Phase 3 - XENOTHREAT INCURSION (5 stages, space): Join a large fleet engagement against multiple Hammerheads and a swarm of escort ships; each pilot must accept the mission individually, and scoring at least one kill counts toward completing the stage.

Phase 4a - XENOTHREAT SURVEILLANCE (3 stages, space): Destroy a set number of XT ships, then EVA to a comm-relay structure, clear a path through laid mines, and start a data-recovery upload while prioritizing disruptive Vanguard attackers.

Phase 4b - EMERGENCY AT KAREAH (1 stage, FPS): A timed assault on Security Post Kareah - stop a hacking device in the central computer room, retrieve a TigerClaw and insert it to begin an upload, then defend the terminal against waves of XenoThreat troops while one player watches the upload screen for interruptions.
Phase 5 - SUPPLY REQUEST (1 stage, FPS): Recover three supply packages from a wreck site guarded by hostile NPCs and deliver them to the drop point to close out the chain.
rewards: Cross-chassis upgrade token converting an Anvil F7C Hornet Mk II into the military F7A Hornet Mk II
Seven-day free rental of the F7A Hornet Mk II
XenoThreat Medium Armor set: ORC-mkX Helmet (Modified), ORC-mkX Core (Modified), ORC-mkX Arms (Modified), and ORC-mkX Legs (Modified)
Rewards were granted only after completing every phase, and were distributed once XenoThreat was defeated (around May / the Alpha 3.23.0 launch)
hazards: Bunker stages feature hostile turrets and dense XT infantry - the server-room/cooling puzzle requires reading codes correctly under fire, and a botched or interrupted upload can fail the stage
Space phases pit you against capital-class Hammerheads plus large numbers of fighters; underpowered ships or weak shields will be overwhelmed
Vanguard ships actively disrupt the comm-relay upload in the surveillance phase and must be dealt with first
The Kareah phase is timed and defended against repeated enemy waves - losing the upload screen to interruption sets you back
Mines surround the comm-relay structure during EVA and must be cleared before reaching the computer
Missions were not available during the live XenoThreat global event, so the full chain had to be finished beforehand to qualify for rewards
steps: Open your mobiGlas and accept the mission 'Overdrive Initiative: INTEL RAID' to begin the chain.

Complete all stages of each phase in order: Phase 1 Intel Raid, Phase 2 Priority Targets, Phase 3 XenoThreat Incursion, Phase 4 (XenoThreat Surveillance then Emergency at Kareah), and Phase 5 Supply Request.
In ship phases, each pilot accepts the mission individually; in fleet battles, securing at least one kill counts toward stage credit.
Finish the entire chain before the live XenoThreat event opened, since the Overdrive missions were pulled once the global assault began.
Rewards were issued automatically after XenoThreat was defeated, around the Alpha 3.23.0 / May window.
tips: This is a group-oriented chain - community guides recommend a minimum of about three players, and a full run took roughly six hours.
Bring combat-capable ships with upgraded quantum drives, strong shields, and heavy weapons for the Hammerhead and turret fights; suggested picks included the Redeemer, Corsair, and Ares Ion/Inferno.
Pack a dedicated support/medical ship such as a C8R or Cutlass Red so the team can heal and recover between stages.
Assign one player to monitor upload/server screens during the bunker, surveillance, and Kareah objectives so interruptions are caught immediately.
Prioritize disruptive enemies (Vanguards at the comm-relay, hackers at Kareah) before chipping away at the rest of the wave.
Complete the chain early - waiting risked the missions being removed once the live XenoThreat event went live.

entries.name: Blockade Runner
entries.patch: Alpha 3.24 (also ran during Pirate Week, September 2024)
entries.location: Stanton system (contested space stations); conceived as a replacement for the Nine Tails Lockdown dynamic event
entries.overview: Blockade Runner was a dynamic, server-wide event introduced with Alpha 3.24 that blended cargo hauling with ship combat — Cloud Imperium Games described it internally as a "space-trucking simulator with teeth." Instead of a pure combat fight, it tasked players with breaking a blockade: flying a cargo-capable ship into a heavily guarded station, retrieving a specific commodity (the volatile reactant Zeta-Prolanide), and hauling it back out to another defended location while avoiding interception. The event grew directly out of how players naturally split roles during XenoThreat — some flying escort/defense while others moved freight — so it was designed to reward cooperation between combat pilots and haulers rather than combat alone. The antagonist force was the Nine Tails pirate syndicate, whose cargo haulers ferried the goods players had to recover. A key design wrinkle: CIG advised disabling enemy cargo haulers rather than destroying them, because intact ships meant more commodity boxes recovered and therefore larger rewards. The event was time-limited and recurring (it returned for Pirate Week and was available "for players on both sides of the law" through September 30, 2024) and concluded with the 3.24 cycle; it is not part of the current Alpha 4.8.x live build.
objectives: Receive the event prompt / pick up the Blockade Runner priority mission once the event spins up on the server
Fly a cargo-capable ship to the contested pickup station and recover the target commodity (Zeta-Prolanide) being moved by Nine Tails forces
Where possible, disable rather than destroy enemy cargo haulers so their cargo boxes survive and can be collected
Load the recovered Zeta-Prolanide aboard your hauler
Run the blockade and deliver the cargo to the designated drop-off location while evading or fighting off interceptors
Repeat delivery runs to accumulate rewards, which scaled with the volume of commodities successfully delivered
rewards: Reward payouts scaled with the amount of commodity successfully recovered and delivered — more cargo brought back meant more reward, which is why disabling (not destroying) enemy haulers was encouraged
Participation/standing rewards tied to the dynamic event and the lawful vs. unlawful side the player chose
Specific aUEC and item reward figures were not consistently published in the text sources reviewed; exact numbers are omitted to avoid fabrication
hazards: Zeta-Prolanide is a volatile industrial reactant that must be kept electrically charged/in a powered stabilizer; if it destabilizes it can undergo a rapid exothermic reaction, so it cannot simply sit in cargo indefinitely (per community guides, manage stabilization during transport)
Both the pickup station and the delivery location were heavily guarded — running the blockade meant facing Nine Tails defenders and the risk of interception or being captured/destroyed mid-run
Open to lawful and unlawful players simultaneously, so PvP interference from opposing players was an inherent risk
Launch-window instability: the event was buggy at release and during its Pirate Week return at least one player publicly demonstrated a way to break/exploit it, so behavior could be inconsistent
tips: Bring a dedicated cargo ship with enough SCU; Zeta-Prolanide ships in seven container sizes (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, 32 SCU), so plan hold space around the box sizes you expect to recover
Coordinate as a group: pair haulers with combat escorts, mirroring the XenoThreat division of labor the event was built to reward
Use disabling tactics (e.g. distortion/EMP-style weapons) on enemy cargo haulers instead of blowing them up — intact wrecks/ships yield more recoverable commodity and bigger payouts
Optimize and pre-plan your route between the guarded pickup and the guarded drop-off to minimize exposure time while the volatile cargo is aboard
Treat the cargo as a clock: keep Zeta-Prolanide powered/stabilized and move efficiently rather than lingering in contested space

entries.name: Alliance Aid: Clearing the Air
entries.location: Persistent Universe — crisis centered on Levski (Delamar), with contributing missions spread across Stanton, Nyx and Pyro
entries.overview: A large-scale, patch-long charity-style event run by the relief organization Alliance Aid (backed by the People's Alliance / UEE) during Alpha 4.6. In the fiction, residents and visitors at Levski had been falling ill for weeks with a mysterious sickness nicknamed the "People's Ailment," later traced to a fungal mold growing inside faulty air filters sold by Gyson Inc. Players "do their part" by completing ordinary PU contracts whose payouts also award event points toward shared reward tracks. It was designed to support nearly every playstyle — haulers, miners, combat pilots and solo players — by drawing on many standard mission archetypes rather than a single scripted instance. A notable change from earlier events: every group member receives 100% of the event points (not a split), explicitly to encourage teaming up. The event launched with patch 4.6 going live on 29 January 2026 and ran across most of the cycle before concluding (community-reported end around 17 March 2026).
objectives: Accumulate event points by completing qualifying PU contracts; points roughly scale with the aUEC payout but are not a 1:1 conversion.
Fill three parallel reward tracks/branches: Transport (hauling/courier), Collection (resource gathering/mining), and Defense/Mercenary (combat, escort, recovery).
Hit per-track point milestones (community-reported ~10,000 points per branch) to unlock that branch's cosmetics.
Reach the overall point total (community-reported ~30,000 points) to unlock the grand prizes.

Complete enough of a given mission type to unlock its milestone / priority mission (e.g. ~25 Hauling-type contracts unlocks a VIP urgent-care cryopod transport to microTech; ~25 and ~50 Combat-type contracts unlock special target / VIP-rescue priority missions).
steps: Travel to / operate around event hubs and accept event-flagged contracts from the contract manager (mobiGlas). Missions appear throughout the PU; those offered in Nyx and Pyro differ in composition from Stanton ones but feed the same shared tracks.

Pick a branch that suits your ship and playstyle. Transport: cargo hauls ranging from tiny ~6 SCU local runs (~40k aUEC) up to 600+ SCU interstellar hauls; providers/flavor include couriers and freight contractors. Collection: gather and deliver resources such as mushrooms, borase, beexalite and lonite (some, like borase, must be mined rather than bought) from spots like Levski's tunnels and Pyro asteroid fields. Defense/Mercenary: clear NPC raiders, hunt supply thieves/hauler-hunters, and escort/cover NPC transports docking at remote asteroid bases.
Grind contracts to bank points; small, fast cargo runs were widely reported as the most points-per-minute route, while the defense branch was the most grind-heavy.
As you clear enough of a single mission type, accept the unlocked milestone/priority mission for that archetype (e.g. the VIP cryopod transport after enough hauling).
Cross each track's milestone to claim its cosmetic rewards, then push the overall total for the grand prizes.
rewards: Per-branch 'division' jacket awarded at the first/early tier of each track (reported around 10% / first milestone).
Transport branch cosmetics: an Alliance Aid MaxLift heavy tractor-beam skin and a matching ARGO RAFT livery.
Defense/Mercenary branch cosmetics: themed weapon/cannon skins and an Aegis Paladin livery.
Medical-themed cosmetics on the overall track: an RSI Apollo Medivac livery (reported ~90%) and a branded ParaMed medical device skin.

Grand prizes for completing the overall track (~30,000 points / 100%): an Aegis Perseus 'Alliance Aid' camo livery and a branded hangar item Fabricator; a Chiron / light-armor set is also reported among the top rewards.
Permanent, account-bound items — the cosmetics and fabricator persist beyond the event.
Standard mission payouts in aUEC on top of event points (individual contracts reported up to roughly 588,000 aUEC for the largest hauls).
hazards: Combat / defense contracts feature enemy waves armed with distortion weapons that can knock out your ship's power plant — bring shields/redundancy and a wingman.
Large cargo hauls mean valuable freight at risk; pirate interdiction and griefing were common on busy event routes.
Resource-gathering objectives can demand mined-only materials (e.g. borase), so 'buy and deliver' shortcuts do not work for every collection contract.
Severe congestion at major landing zones during the event caused long ASOP/landing queues (reported ~20-minute waits).
A reported bug had some missions register as complete without paying out the event points/rewards.
tips: Favor short, repeatable small-SCU hauls for the best points-per-hour; one report cited ~800 points in ~20 minutes versus 45+ minutes for medium loads.
Because everyone in a party gets 100% of the points, group up — it multiplies effective point gain rather than dividing it.
Use a hauling beam/ATLS efficiently; positioning at the right angle to the cargo grid speeds up loading.
Solo players in small ships should stick to entry-level contracts and the Transport/Collection branches; save the Defense branch for groups.
Spread effort across all three tracks if you want the grand prizes, since the overall total is the sum of branch progress.
Plan resource routes with community tools (e.g. UEX trade data and survey/mining references) to find the right ores and best sell stops.
